text:
The TALSPEAK process (Trivalent Actinide Lanthanide Separation with Phosphorus-reagent Extraction from Aqueous Komplexes) was originally developed at Oak Ridge National Laboratory in the 1960s by Weaver and Kappelmann as an alternative to the LiCl-based TRAMEX process. It achieves group separation by selectively extracting trivalent lanthanides into an organic phase while retaining trivalent actinides in an aqueous phase using aminopolycarboxylate complexants. Over several decades, the benchmark TALSPEAK formulation—utilizing HDEHP as the extractant in an aromatic diluent and a concentrated lactate buffer containing DTPA—has become the international standard for advanced 4f/5f partitioning.

While the system is highly efficient under ideal conditions (separating the limiting Am³⁺/Nd³⁺ pair with a single-stage factor of ~100), its industrial adoption has been limited by two primary technical hurdles: the requirement for rigid pH control within a narrow window and relatively sluggish phase-transfer kinetics. Traditionally, high concentrations of lactate buffer (0.5–2.0 M) have been required to overcome these issues. These operational complexities have driven a decade of fundamental research aimed at deconstructing the underlying molecular interactions of the process.

This report reviews recent advances in the understanding of TALSPEAK chemistry, moving beyond empirical observations to detail the thermodynamics and kinetics that govern the system. Key findings include the identification of lactic acid partitioning into the organic phase as a primary driver of the system’s sensitive pH dependence, the discovery of polynuclear mixed-ligand complexes at high metal loadings, and a clarified role for lactate as a phase-transfer catalyst via transient aqueous species. Additionally, we discuss the evolution of "Advanced TALSPEAK" concepts, which utilize more basic phosphonic acid extractants and weaker holdback reagents to simplify the process and eliminate many of its historical drawbacks.
